The first step is to partition the disk into one or more groups of cylinders. The

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 94)
content: operating system can treat each partition as though it were a separate disk. For instance, one partition can hold a copy of the operating system's executable code, while another holds user files. After partitioning, the second step is logical formatting (or creation of a file system). In this step, the operating system stores the initial file -system data structures onto the disk. Boot Block When a co mputer is powered up or rebooted, it needs to have an initial program to run. This initial program is called bootstrap program. It initializes all aspects of the system (i.e. from CPU registers to device controllers and the contents of main memory) and the n starts the operating system. To do its job, the bootstrap program finds the operating system kernel on disk, loads that kernel into memory, and jumps to an initial address to begin the operating -system execution. For most computers, the bootstrap is stored in read -only memory (ROM). This location is convenient, because ROM needs no initialization and is at a fixed location that the processor can start executing when powered up or reset. And since ROM is read only, it cannot be infected by a computer virus. The problem is that changing this bootstrap code requires changing the ROM hardware chips. For this reason, most systems store a tiny bootstrap loader program in the boot ROM, whose only job is to bring in a full bootstrap program from disk. The full bootstrap program can be changed easily: A new version is simply written onto the disk. The full bootstrap program is stored in a partition (at a fixed location on the disk) is called the boot blocks . A disk that has a boot partition is called a boot disk or system disk. Bad Blocks Since disks have

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 95)
content: moving parts and small tolerances, they are prone to failure. Sometimes the failure is complete, and the disk needs to be replaced, and its contents restored from backup media to the new disk. More frequently, one or more sectors become defective. Most disks even come from the factory with bad blocks. Depending on the disk and controller in use, these blocks are handled in a variety of ways. The controller maintains a list of bad blocks on the disk. The list is initialized during the low -level format at the factory, and is updated over the life of the disk. The controller can be told to replace each bad sector logically with one of the spare sectors. This scheme is known as sector sparing or forwardin g. SWAP -SPACE MANAGEMENT Page 84 The swap -space management is a low -level task of the operating system. The main goal for the design and implementation of swap space is to provide the best throughput for the virtual -memory system. Swap -Space Use Swap space is used in various ways by different operating systems depending on the implemented memory -management algorithms. Those systems are implemented swapping, they may use swap space to hold the entire process image, including the code and data segments. The amount of swap space needed on a system can vary depending on the amount of physical memory, Swap -Space Location A swap space can reside in two places: Swap space can be carved out of the normal file system, or it can be in a separate disk partition. If the swap space is simply a large file within the file system, normal file -system routines can be used to create it, name it, and allocate its space. This approach is easy to implement and is also inefficient. Alternatively, swap

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 96)
content: space can be created in a separate disk partition. No file system or directory structure is placed on this space. A separate swap -space storage manager is used to allocate and deallocate the blocks. This manage r uses algorithms optimized for speed and storage efficiency. RAID STRUCTURE A Redundant Array of Inexpensive Disks(RAID) may be used to increase disk reliability. RAID may be implemented in hardware or in the operating system. The RAID consists of seven levels, zero through six. These levels designate different design architectures that share three common characteristics: RAID is a set of physical disk drives viewed by the operating system as a single logical drive. Data are distributed across the physical drives of an array in a scheme known as striping, described subsequently. Redundant disk capacity is used to store parity information, which guarantees data recoverability in case of a disk failure. Page 85 (RAID levels) (Here P indicates error -correcting bits and C indicates a second copy of the data) The RAID levels are described as follows:  RAID Level 0: RAID level 0 refers to disk arrays with striping at the level of blocks, but without any redundancy (such as parity bits). Figure(a) shows an array of size 4.  RAID Level 1: RAID level 1 refers to disk mirroring. Figure (b) shows a mirrored organization that holds four disks' worth of data.  RAID Level 2: RAID level 2 is also known as memory -style error -correcting code (ECC) organization. Each byte in a memory system may have a parity bit associated with it that records whether the numbers of bits in the byte set to 1 is even (parity=O) or odd (parity=l). The idea of ECC can be used directly in disk arrays via striping of bytes across disks.  RAID level

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 97)
content: 3: RAID level 3, or bit-interleaved parity organization, improves on level 2 by noting that, disk controllers can detect whether a sector has been read correctly, so a single parity bit can be used for error correction, as well as for detection. The idea is as follows. If one of the sectors gets damaged, we know exactly which sector it is, and, for each bit in the Page 86 sector, we can figure out whether it is a 1 or a 0 by computing the parity of the corresponding bits from sectors in the other disks. If the parity of the remaining bits is equal to the stored parity, the missing bit is 0; otherwise, it is 1.  RAID Level 4: RAID level 4 or block -interleaved parity organization uses block -level striping, as in RAID 0 and in addition keeps a parity block on a separate disk for corresponding blocks from N other disks. This scheme is shown pictorially in Figure(e). If one of the disks fails, the parity block can be used with the corresponding blocks from the other disks to restore the blocks of the failed disk.  RAID level 5: RAID level 5 or block -interleaved distributed parity is similar as level 4 but level 5 spreading data and parity among all N + 1 disks, rather than storing data in N disks and parity in one disk. For each block, one of the disks stores the parity, and the others store data. By spreading the parity across all the disks in the set, RAID 5 avoids the potential overuse of a single parity disk that can occur with RAID 4.  RAID Level 6: RAID level 6 (is also called the P+Q redundancy scheme) is much like RAID level 5, but stores extra redundant

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 98)
content: information to guard against multiple disk failures. Instead of using parity, error -correcting codes such as the Reed -Solomon codes are used. I/O SYSTEM The role of the operating system in computer I/O is to manage and control I/O operations and I/O devices. A device communicates with a computer system by sending signals over a cable or even through the air. The device communicates with the machine via a connection point. If one or more devices use a common set of wires for communication, the connection is called a bus. When device A has a cable that plugs into device B, and device B has a cable that plugs into device C, and device C plugs into a port on the computer, this arrangement is called a daisy chain. A daisy chain usually operates as a bus. Buses are used widely in computer architecture as follows: Page 87 (A typical PC bus structure) This figure shows a PC1 bus (the common PC system bus) that connects the processor - memory subsystem to the fast devices, and an expansion bus that connects relatively slow devices such as the keyboard and serial and parallel ports. In the upper -right portion of the figure, four disks are connected together on a SCSI bus plugged into a SCSI controller. A controller is a collection of electronics that can operate a port, a bus, or a device. A serial -port controller is a simple device controller. It is a single chip (or portion of a chip) in the computer that controls the signals on the wires of a serial port. Since the SCSI protocol is complex, the SCSI bus controller is often implemented as a separate circuit board (or a host adapter) that plugs into the computer. It contains a processor, microcode, and some private

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 99)
content: memory to enable it to process the SCSI protocol messages. Some devices have their own built -in controllers. This board is the disk controller. It has microcode and a processor to do many tasks, such as bad -sector mapping, prefetching, buffering, and caching. An I/O port consists of four registers, called the status, control, data-in, and data-out registers. The status register contains bits that can be read by the host. These bits indicate states such as whether the current command has completed, whether a byte is available to be read from the data -in register, and whether there has been a device error. The control register can be written by the host to start a command or to change the mode of a device. The data -in register is read by the host to get input. The data -out register is written by the host to send output. The external devices can be grouped into three categories: Page 88 1. Human readable: Suitable for communicating with the computer user. Examples include printers and terminals, the latter consisting of video display, keyboard, and perhaps other devices such as a mouse. 2. Machine readable: Suitable for communicating with elect ronic equipment. Examples are disk drives, USB keys, sensors, controllers, and actuators. 3. Communication: Suitable for communicating with remote devices. Examples are digital line drivers and modems. ORGANIZATION OF THE I/O FUNCTION(I/O Technique) There are three techniques for performing I/O operations: 1. Programmed I/O: The processor issues an I/O command, on behalf of a process, to an I/O module; that process busy waits for the operation to be completed before proceeding. 2. Interrupt -driven I/O: The processor issues an I/O command on behalf of a process. There are two possibilities. If the I/O instruction from the process is non blocking, then the processor

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 100)
content: continues to execute instructions from the process that issued the I/O command. If the I/O instruction is blocking, then the next instruction that the processor executes is from the OS, which will put the current process in a blocked state and schedule another process. 3. Direct memory access (DMA): A DMA module controls the exchange of data between main memory and an I/O module. The processor sends a request for the transfer of a block of data to the DMA module and is interrupted only after the entire block has been transferred. Interrupt -driven I/O The basic interrupt mechanism works as follows. The CPU hardware has a wire called the interrupt -request line that the CPU senses after executing every instruction. When the CPU detects that a controller has asserted a signal on the interrupt request line, the CPU saves a small amount of state, such as the current value of the instruction pointer, and jumps to the interrupt -handler routine at a fixed address in memory. The interrupt handler determines the cause of the interrupt, performs the necessary processing, and executes a return from interrupt instruction to return the CPU to the execution state prior to the interrupt. The above mechanism concludes that the device controller raises an interrupt by asserting a signal on the interrupt request line, the CPU catches the interrupt and dispatches to the interrupt handler, and the handler clears the interrupt by servicing the device. Page 89 (Diagram of Interrupt -driven I/O cycle) In a modern operating system, we need more sophisticated interrupt -handling features as follows:  First, we need the ability to defer interrupt handling during critical processing.  Second, we need an efficient way to dispatch to the proper interrupt handler for a device.  Third, we need multilevel interrupts, so that

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 101)
content: the operating system can distinguish between high - and low-priority interrupts, and can respond with the appropriate degree of urgency. In modern computer hardware, these three features are provided by the CPU and by the interrupt -controller hardware. Direct Memory Access The DMA unit is capable of mimicking the processor and of taking over control of the system bus just like a processor. It needs to do this to transfer data to and from memory over the system bus. The DMA technique works as follows:  When the processor wishes to read or write a block of data, it issues a command to the DMA module by sending to the DMA module the following information:  Whether a read or write is requested, using the read or write control line between the processor and the DMA module. Page 90  The address of the I/O device involved, communicated on the data lines.  The starting location in memory to read from or write to, communicated on the data lines and stored by the DMA module in its address register.  The number of words to be read or written, again communicated via the data lines and stored in the data count register.  Then the processor continues with other work. It has delegated this I/O operation to the DMA module.  The DMA module transfers the entire block of data, one word at a time, directly to or from memory, without going through the processor.  When the transfer is complete, the DMA module sends an interrupt signal to the processor. Thus, the processor is involved only at the beginning and end of the transfer. (Steps in a DMA transfer) APPLICATION I/O INTERFACE I/O system calls encapsulate device behaviors in generic classes. Each general kind is accessed through a standardized

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 102)
content: set of functions, called an interface. The differences are encapsulated in kernel modules called device drivers that internally are custom tailored to each device, but that export one of the standard interfaces. Devices vary in many dimensions: o Character -stream or block o Sequential or random -access o Sharable or dedicated o Speed of operation o read -write, read only, or write only Page 91 (Diagram of a kernel I/O structure) The purpose of the device -driver layer is to hide the differences among device controllers from the I/O subsystem of the kernel, much as the I/O system calls encapsulate the behavior of devices in a few generic classes that hide hardware differences from applications. Making the I/O subsystem independent of the hardware simplifies th e job of the operating -system developer. It also benefits the hardware manufacturers. They either design new devices to be compatible with an existing host controller interface (such as SCSI -2), or they write device drivers to interface the new hardware to popular operating systems. Thus, new peripherals can be attached to a computer without waiting for the operating -system vendor to develop support code. KERNEL I/O SUBSYSTEM Kernels provide many services related to I/O. Several services (i.e. scheduling, buffering, caching, spooling, device reservation and error handling) are provided by the kernel's I/O subsystem and build on the hardware and device driver infrastructure. 1. I/O Scheduling: It is used to schedule a set of I/O requests that means to determine a good order in which to execute them. Scheduling can improve overall system performance and can reduce the average waiting time for I/O to complete. 2. Buffering : A buffer is a memory area that stores data while they are transferred between two devices or between a device and an application. Buffering is done for

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 103)
content: three reasons.  One reason is to cope with a speed mismatch between the producer and consumer of a data stream. Page 92  A second use of buffering is to adapt between devices that have different data -transfer sizes.  A third use of buffering is to support copy semantics for application I/O. 3. Caching: A cache is a region of fast memory that holds copies of data. Access to the cached copy is more efficient than access to the original. Cache is used to improve the I/O efficiency for files that are shared by applications or that are being written and reread rapidly. The difference between a buffer and a cache is that a buffer may hold the only existing copy of a data item, whereas a cache just holds a copy on faster storage of an item that resides elsewhere. Caching and buffering are distinct functions, but sometimes a region of memory can be used for both purposes. 4. Spooling : A spool is a buffer that holds output for a device, such as a printer, that cannot accept interleaved data streams. Although a printer can serve only one job at a time, several applications may wish to print their output concurrently, without having their output mixed together. The operating system solves this problem by intercepting all output to the printer. Each application's output is spooled to a separate disk file. When an application finishes printing, the spooling system queues the corresponding spool file for output to the printer. The spooling system copies the queued spool files to the printer one at a time. The operating system provides a control interface that enables users and system administrators to display the queue, to remove unwanted jobs before those jobs print, to suspend printing while the printer is serviced,

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 104)
content: and so on. 5. Device Reservation: It provides support for exclusive device access, by enabling a process to allocate an idle device, and to deallocate that device when it is no longer needed. Many operating systems provide functions that enable processes to coordinate exclusive access among them. It watches out for deadlock to avoid. Transforming I/O request to Hardware Operations Consider reading a file from disk for a process:  Determine device holding file  Translate name to device representation  Physically read data from disk into buffer  Make data available to requesting process  Return control to process The following steps are described the lifecycle of a blocking read request: 1. A process issues a blocking read() system call to a file descriptor of a file that has been opened previously. 2. The system -call code in the kernel checks the parameters for correctness. In the case of input, if the data are already av ailable in the buffer cache, the data are returned to the process and the I/O request is completed. 3. Otherwise, a physical I/O needs to be performed, so the process is removed from the run queue and is placed on the wait queue for the device, and the I/O re quest is scheduled. Eventually, the I/O subsystem sends the request to the device driver. Depending on the operating system, the request is sent via a subroutine call or via an in-kernel message. Page 93 4. The device driver allocates kernel buffer space to receive the data, and schedules the I/O. Eventually, the driver sends commands to the device controller by writing into the device control registers. 5. The device controller operates the device hardware to perform the data transfer. 6. The driver may poll for status and data, or it may have set

title: Excerpt from OS_NOTES_TEST.pdf (Part 105)
content: up a DMA transfer into kernel memory. We assume that the transfer is managed by a DMA controller, which generates an interrupt when the transfer completes. 7. The correct interrupt handler receives the interrupt via the interrupt -vector table, stores any necessary data, signals the device driver, and returns from the interrupt. 8. The device driver receives the signal, determines which I/O request completed, determines the request's status, and signals the kernel I/O subsystem that the request has been completed. 9. The kernel transfers data or returns codes to the address space of the requesting process, and moves the process from the wait queue back to the ready queue. 10. Moving the process to the ready queue unblocks the process. When the scheduler assigns the process to the CPU, the process resumes execution at the completion of the system call. (The life cycle of an I/O request)

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 1)
content: Introduction to Customer Centricity Customer centricity is a strategic business approach that places the customer at the heart of an organization's operations, decision-making, and value proposition. It's a fundamental shift from the traditional product-centric model, where the focus is on developing and selling products, to a customer-centric approach where the goal is to understand and meet the evolving needs and expectations of the customer. By adopting a customer-centric mindset, companies can build stronger relationships with their customers, increase loyalty, and drive sustainable growth. At the core of customer centricity is a deep understanding of the customer - their pain points, preferences, behaviors, and journeys. This knowledge is then used to design products, services, and experiences that truly resonate with the customer, ultimately leading to increased satisfaction, advocacy, and long-term profitability. Customer centricity requires a holistic approach that encompasses the entire organization, from marketing and sales to operations and customer service. Deadly sin #1: Failing to truly understand your customers 1 Lack of Customer Empathy Many businesses fall into the trap of making assumptions about their customers without taking the time to truly understand their needs, pain points, and desires. This lack of empathy can lead to products, services, and experiences that miss the mark and fail to resonate with the target audience. Successful customer-centric organizations make a concerted effort to walk in their customers' shoes, leveraging research, data, and direct feedback to gain a deep, multidimensional understanding of who their customers are and what matters most to them. 2 Disconnected Customer Insights Even when businesses collect customer data and feedback, they often struggle to synthesize and act on those insights in a meaningful way. Siloed data, poor communication between departments, and a failure to translate insights into concrete strategies can result in a disconnect between what customers want and what

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 2)
content: the business delivers. Overcoming this requires a more holistic, cross-functional approach to customer intelligence, where insights are seamlessly shared and leveraged to drive business decisions and customer-centric innovation. 3 Lack of Continuous Listening Customer needs and preferences are constantly evolving, yet many businesses treat customer research as a one-time exercise. Failing to maintain an ongoing dialogue with customers and continuously gather their feedback can lead to outdated, incomplete, or inaccurate understandings. Truly customer-centric organizations make customer listening a core, continuous practice, actively seeking out customer input through surveys, focus groups, social media, and other channels to stay attuned to shifting customer expectations and behaviors. Deadly Sin #2: Prioritizing Short-Term Gains Over Long-Term Relationships In the relentless pursuit of growth and profitability, it can be tempting for businesses to prioritize short-term gains over the cultivation of lasting customer relationships. This "deadly sin" of customer centricity can have disastrous consequences, eroding trust, loyalty, and long-term value. By focusing solely on immediate metrics like quarterly sales or monthly active users, companies risk making decisions that please shareholders in the near-term but alienate customers in the long run. They may offer promotions or discounts to drive quick conversions, but fail to invest in truly understanding and meeting the evolving needs of their client base. The most successful, customer-centric organizations recognize that building strong, mutually beneficial relationships requires patience, empathy, and a willingness to forego short-term gains in favor of cultivating lifelong brand advocates. This means resisting the urge to maximize profits at all costs, and instead striking a balance between financial prudence and a genuine, sustained commitment to the customer experience. Deadly sin #3: Treating all customers the same One of the most common and detrimental mistakes companies make in the pursuit of customer centricity is treating all of their customers the same. The

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 3)
content: reality is that your customer base is made up of diverse individuals, each with their own unique needs, preferences, and behaviors. Attempting to apply a one-size-fits-all approach simply won't work in today's competitive landscape. Failing to segment and personalize: By not segmenting your customer base and tailoring your approach to different segments, you miss out on opportunities to truly meet the specific needs of each group. Some customers may value speed and convenience, while others prioritize personalized service and exclusive offerings. Ignoring customer lifetime value: Not all customers are equally valuable to your business. Some may generate significantly more revenue and profit over time than others. Treating high-value customers the same as low-value ones is a costly mistake that can erode your bottom line. Neglecting customer loyalty: When you treat all customers the same, you fail to recognize and reward your most loyal and engaged customers. This can lead to frustration and a higher likelihood of those valuable customers seeking out your competitors. To overcome this deadly sin, organizations must invest in robust customer data and analytics capabilities to deeply understand their customer base. This allows them to segment customers, personalize experiences, and allocate resources based on each customer's unique value and needs. By embracing the diversity of your customer base, you can unlock new levels of customer satisfaction, loyalty, and profitability. Relying too heavily on technology over human interaction In the quest for efficiency and automation, organizations can sometimes become overly reliant on technology at the expense of genuine human connection. While digital tools and platforms can streamline certain processes, they should not completely replace the personal touch that is so vital to cultivating strong customer relationships. Customers often crave that human element - the empathetic ear, the personalized recommendation, the reassuring voice on the other end of the

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 4)
content: line. When technology dominates the customer experience, it can leave individuals feeling isolated, unheard, and disconnected from the brand they're trying to engage with. This can erode trust, damage loyalty, and undermine the very customer-centricity that organizations strive to achieve. True customer centricity requires finding the right balance between technological efficiency and human interaction. Automating routine tasks and queries can free up customer service representatives to focus on more complex, high-touch engagements. Blending digital and in-person touchpoints can create a seamless, omnichannel experience. But at the core, businesses must never forget the power of a genuine human connection in building lasting, meaningful relationships with their customers. Deadly Sin #5: Focusing on Metrics Over Customer Satisfaction Misaligned Priorities In the relentless pursuit of growth and profitability, many companies become overly fixated on vanity metrics like sales figures, web traffic, and conversion rates. While these metrics can provide a high-level view of performance, they often fail to capture the true health of the customer relationship. By prioritizing these numbers over genuine customer satisfaction, companies risk making short-sighted decisions that erode long-term loyalty and advocacy. Disconnect from the Customer Experience When organizations are laser-focused on hitting arbitrary targets, they can lose sight of the actual customer experience. Frontline employees may feel pressured to optimize for metrics rather than fully addressing customer needs and concerns. This can lead to a disconnect between the company's priorities and the customer's lived experience, resulting in frustration, churn, and a damaged brand reputation. Overlooking Qualitative Insights Quantitative metrics can provide a valuable perspective, but they often fail to capture the nuanced, qualitative aspects of the customer journey. By neglecting to collect and act on customer feedback, sentiment data, and anecdotal insights, companies miss out on a rich understanding of their customers' pain points, motivations, and emotional responses. This

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 5)
content: shortsightedness can blind them to emerging trends and opportunities for meaningful innovation. Deadly sin #6: Siloing customer data and insights Data Silos One of the deadliest sins of customer centricity is the tendency to silo customer data across different departments and systems. This fragmented approach makes it nearly impossible to get a comprehensive, 360-degree view of the customer, hindering your ability to deliver seamless, personalized experiences. Disconnected Insights When customer data is siloed, insights about customer behavior, preferences, and pain points become fragmented as well. Teams struggle to uncover the full picture, leading to suboptimal decision-making and missed opportunities to better serve customers. Lack of Collaboration Siloed customer data often leads to a lack of cross-functional collaboration, as different departments operate in their own bubbles. This prevents the organization from leveraging the collective intelligence and expertise needed to truly understand and cater to customer needs. Unified Platform To overcome this deadly sin, organizations must invest in a unified customer data platform that consolidates information from various sources. This empowers teams to access a comprehensive, real-time view of the customer, enabling them to make data-driven decisions and deliver personalized, consistent experiences across all touchpoints. Deadly sin #7: Neglecting employee engagement and empowerment 1 Disempowered staff Employees feeling powerless to help customers 2 Lack of training Insufficient investment in upskilling teams 3 Disconnected leadership Executives out of touch with frontline challenges One of the most pernicious customer centricity sins is neglecting the engagement and empowerment of your own employees. When frontline staff feel disempowered, disengaged, or disconnected from your organization's mission, it creates a disconnect between your brand promises and the actual customer experience. Truly customer-centric companies understand that their employees are the face of the brand and the conduit through which the customer experience is delivered. Investing in robust training programs,

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 6)
content: providing clear escalation paths, and fostering a culture of autonomy and ownership are critical to enabling staff to be responsive, creative, and empowered to delight customers. Additionally, maintaining open communication channels between leadership and frontline teams is essential. Executives who are out of tour with the day-to-day challenges faced by customer-facing employees will struggle to make decisions that truly put the customer first. By involving staff in the strategic planning process and soliciting their feedback, companies can build a shared sense of purpose and empower employees to be active champions of the customer experience. Deadly sin #8: Lacking a cohesive customer-centric strategy One of the most insidious "deadly sins" of customer centricity is the lack of a cohesive, organization-wide strategy that truly puts the customer at the center of all decision-making. Far too often, companies approach customer experience in a piecemeal, siloed manner, with different departments and teams pursuing their own initiatives without alignment or a unifying vision. This lack of strategic focus can manifest in a number of ways - from confusing and inconsistent branding and messaging across touchpoints, to disjointed customer journeys that leave individuals feeling like they're being passed from one silo to the next. Without a holistic plan that permeates the entire organization, efforts to be "customer-centric" often fall flat, as employees struggle to operationalize the concept and translate it into meaningful action. Characteristic of a Cohesive Customer-Centric Strategy Characteristic of a Siloed, Fragmented Approach Clear, shared vision of the ideal customer experience Disconnected, department-specific initiatives Alignment of goals, metrics, and incentives across the organization Conflicting priorities and misaligned KPIs Empowered, cross-functional collaboration Territorial, siloed decision-making Continuous customer feedback loop informing all aspects of the business Sporadic, one-off customer research To avoid this deadly sin, organizations must take a hard look at their strategic planning process,

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 7)
content: breaking down internal silos and aligning all functions and teams around a shared vision of customer centricity. This requires strong leadership, a customer-first mindset, and a willingness to challenge traditional ways of operating. Only then can a company truly transform into an insights-driven, customer-obsessed enterprise. Conclusion: Embracing true customer centricity To truly embrace customer centricity, organizations must move beyond the "deadly sins" and adopt a holistic, long-term approach that puts the customer at the heart of everything they do. This begins with a deep understanding of your customers - their needs, pain points, and aspirations. Only then can you tailor your products, services, and interactions to deliver exceptional, personalized experiences that build lasting loyalty and trust. Empowering your employees to be customer advocates is also crucial. Fostering a culture of empathy, transparency, and responsiveness ensures your frontline staff can quickly resolve issues and anticipate customer needs. Integrating customer data and insights across siloed departments is key to maintaining a 360-degree view and fueling continuous improvement. Ultimately, true customer centricity is a mindset, not just a set of tactics. It requires aligning your entire organization around a shared mission of creating remarkable experiences that delight and inspire your customers. By embracing this holistic, customer-first approach, you can drive sustainable growth, uncover new opportunities, and cement your position as an industry leader. The path to customer centricity may not be easy, but the rewards for those who get it right are immense. THANK YOU

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-II.pptx (Part 1)
content: The Transformative Power of Customer Centricity At the heart of any successful business lies a fundamental commitment to putting customers first. Customer centricity is more than just a buzzword - it's a strategic approach that has the power to transform an organization, driving innovation, fostering loyalty, and ultimately, fueling sustained growth. In this comprehensive exploration, we'll delve into the core principles of customer centricity, unveiling the profound benefits it can unlock and the key components that make it a transformative force in today's competitive landscape. The Benefits of Customer Centricity Enhanced Customer Loyalty When organizations prioritize the needs and preferences of their customers, they cultivate a deep sense of trust and loyalty. Customers feel valued, appreciated, and more inclined to remain loyal to a brand that consistently delivers exceptional experiences. Increased Revenue and Profitability Customer-centric businesses often enjoy higher customer lifetime value, as satisfied customers are more likely to make repeat purchases and refer others. This translates into sustainable revenue growth and improved profitability over the long term. Competitive Advantage In today's crowded marketplace, customer centricity can be a powerful differentiator. By truly understanding and catering to the unique needs of their target audience, organizations can set themselves apart from the competition and become the preferred choice for consumers. Key Components of Customer Centricity 1 Comprehensive Customer Understanding Successful customer-centric organizations invest heavily in understanding their customers' needs, pain points, preferences, and behaviors. This deep understanding enables them to tailor their offerings and experiences to meet the unique requirements of each customer segment. 2 Seamless, Personalized Experiences Customer-centric companies strive to deliver seamless, personalized experiences across all touchpoints, from initial awareness to post-purchase support. By anticipating and addressing customer needs at every stage, they foster a sense of trust and loyalty. 3 Agile, Data-Driven Decision Making Customer-centric organizations leverage data-driven

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-II.pptx (Part 2)
content: insights to continuously optimize their strategies, products, and services. They foster a culture of agility, where rapid experimentation and data-informed decisions drive ongoing improvements to the customer experience. 4 Empowered, Customer-Focused Employees Cultivating a customer-centric culture requires empowering employees to prioritize the needs of customers. This includes providing them with the necessary training, resources, and decision-making authority to deliver exceptional experiences. Understanding Your Customers 1 Customer Research Conduct in-depth market research to gather insights into your customers' demographics, psychographics, behaviors, pain points, and preferences. This information forms the foundation for a customer-centric strategy. 2 Persona Development Create detailed customer personas that represent your target audience. These personas help you tailor your products, services, and experiences to the specific needs and desires of each segment. 3 Continuous Feedback Loops Establish ongoing feedback channels, such as surveys, focus groups, and customer interactions, to continuously learn about your customers' evolving needs and preferences. This allows you to respond quickly and adapt your approach accordingly. Designing Customer-Centric Experiences Journey Mapping Map out the complete customer journey, identifying key touchpoints and pain points. This holistic view enables you to design seamless, end-to-end experiences that address customer needs at every stage. Personalization and Customization Leverage data and technology to personalize the customer experience, tailoring product recommendations, content, and interactions to individual preferences and behaviors. Omnichannel Integration Ensure a consistent, seamless experience across all channels, both physical and digital, allowing customers to engage with your brand on their preferred platforms. Empowering Your Employees Comprehensive Training Provide thorough training to equip your employees with the knowledge, skills, and tools necessary to deliver exceptional customer experiences. Empowered Decision-Making Empower your frontline employees to make decisions that prioritize customer needs, fostering a sense of ownership and accountability. Cross-Functional Collaboration Encourage cross-functional collaboration, enabling employees to work together to identify and

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-13_Reference-Material-II.pptx (Part 3)
content: address customer pain points. Reward and Recognition Implement a system that recognizes and rewards employees who consistently deliver outstanding customer experiences. Measuring Customer Centricity Metric Description Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) Measure how satisfied customers are with your products, services, and overall experience. Net Promoter Score (NPS) Gauge the likelihood of customers to recommend your brand to others. Customer Retention Rate Track the percentage of customers who continue to do business with your company over time. Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Estimate the total revenue a customer is expected to generate during their relationship with your brand. First Contact Resolution (FCR) Measure the percentage of customer inquiries resolved on the first interaction. By regularly monitoring and analyzing these key metrics, organizations can gain valuable insights into the effectiveness of their customer-centric strategies and make data-driven decisions to continuously improve the customer experience. Sustaining a Customer-Centric Culture Lead by Example Senior leadership must model and champion a customer-centric mindset, ensuring that it permeates throughout the entire organization. Ongoing Training and Development Provide continuous training and development opportunities to keep employees up-to-date on customer trends, best practices, and emerging technologies. Embrace Feedback and Iteration Foster a culture of continuous improvement, where customer feedback is actively sought, and adjustments are made to enhance the customer experience. Celebrate Customer-Centric Successes Recognize and celebrate employees and teams who go above and beyond in delivering exceptional customer experiences, reinforcing the importance of customer centricity. By embedding customer centricity into the very fabric of the organization, companies can create a sustainable, customer-centric culture that drives long-term growth and success. THANK YOU

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 1)
content: DECISION MAKING In simple terms, decision making is the process of making choices by recognizing the problem, gathering information about feasible solutions, and finalizing the best alternative. This process is carried out through an intuitive or logical process, or a combination of two. Intuition is all about using your gut feeling to take a stand on the possible course of action. In contrast, a logical process uses facts and figures to make scientifically sound decisions. AGENDA Essence of making decision, Importance of Decision making Types of decision, Decision Making - Conditions Effective Decision Making, Scenarios 1.Essence of making decision Identifying the Problem or Opportunity Decision-making starts when you recognize that something needs to change or be chosen. Gathering Relevant Information You collect facts, data, opinions, and insights needed to make a wise choice. Evaluating Alternatives You compare different options based on: Pros and cons Risks Costs Expected outcomes Alignment with goals Predicting Consequences A good decision maker thinks about what will happen after each choice. Choosing the Best Option You select the alternative that offers the maximum benefit with minimum risk. Taking Responsibility You commit to the choice and act on it. Reviewing the Outcome You learn from what worked and what didn’t—this improves future decisions. In short: Decision-making is about choosing wisely by analyzing information, understanding consequences, and picking the most beneficial option. 2.Importance of Decision making 1. Goal Achievement: Effective decisions help managers set and achieve choosing the best course of action. organizational goals by Resource Utilization: Good decision making ensures optimal use of resources such as time, money, and manpower, minimizing waste Problem Solving: Managers face various challenges; decision making helps in identifying problems and selecting appropriate solutions Adaptability: Decision making enables organizations to adapt to changes in the environment by making timely and appropriate adjustments. Coordination

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 2)
content: and Control: Decisions help in coordinating activities across departments and controlling operations to maintain efficiency. Motivation and Leadership: Involving employees in decision making can boost morale and foster a sense of ownership and commitment. Risk Management: Through careful decision making, managers can anticipate risks and take preventive measures to avoid potential losses THANK YOU

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-II.pptx (Part 1)
content: Types of decision A. Based on Nature Programmed Decisions Routine, repetitive, structured. Follow established procedures or rules. Example: Handling customer complaints, reordering stock. Non-Programmed Decisions New, unstructured, require judgment & creativity. No predefined rules. Example: Entering a new market, launching a new product. B. Based on Importance Strategic Decisions Long-term, future-oriented, taken by top management. High risk and impact. Example: Business expansion, mergers. Tactical Decisions Medium-term, taken by middle management. Convert strategies into actions. Example: Allocating budgets, setting departmental goals. Operational Decisions Day-to-day, short-term decisions by lower management. Ensure smooth functioning. Example: work scheduling, task assignments. C. Based on Participation Individual Decisions Taken by a single person. Faster but may lack diversity of ideas. Group Decisions Taken collectively. More ideas, higher acceptance, but time-consuming. D. Based on Situations Routine Decisions Regular, common issues. Less analysis required. Emergency Decisions Quick decisions needed in urgent situations. High pressure and risk. Decision Making - Conditions Decision-making occurs under three major conditions: Decision-Making Under Certainty The outcome of each alternative is known. Complete information is available. Very low risk. Example: Fixed deposit returns, machine capacity planning. Decision-Making Under Risk Alternatives and outcomes are known, but probabilities are uncertain. Some information is available, but not complete. Managers use forecasting, past data, and probability. Example: Investment decisions, demand prediction. Decision-Making Under Uncertainty Outcomes are completely unknown. No data, no probabilities—high ambiguity. Decisions rely on intuition, judgment, and experience. Example: Introducing a product in a brand-new market. THANK YOU

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-III.pptx (Part 1)
content: Effective Decision Making With Real-World Scenarios What is Effective Decision Making? Process of choosing the best option from available alternatives Involves logic, analysis, judgment, and experience Helps solve problems, reduce risk, and achieve goals Essential for managers, students, teams, and leaders Key Features of Effective Decision Making Clear understanding of the problem Use of accurate and relevant information Evaluating multiple alternatives Considering risks and consequences Logical and unbiased thinking Timely decision making Reviewing outcomes for improvement Steps in Effective Decision Making Identify the problem Gather necessary information Generate alternatives Evaluate pros and cons Choose the best option Implement the decision Review and learn from the results SCENERIOS Scenario 1 – Business Decision Situation: A company wants to launch a new product. Decision Factors: Market demand Competitor analysis Budget and resources Risk level Effective Decision: Launch in the most promising market segment after analyzing data. Scenario 2 – Career Decision Situation: A student must choose between job or higher studies. Steps Used: Identify goals (salary, learning, long-term growth) Compare benefits and drawbacks Seek mentor guidance Effective Decision: Choose the option that aligns with future career vision. Scenario 3 – Emergency Decision Situation: System failure during peak business hours. Decision Factors: • • • Response time Available backup plans Impact on users Effective Decision: Activate backup system immediately and fix issue later. THANK YOU

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-IV.pptx (Part 1)
content: PLANNING Key Components • Nature • Levels Structure • Design WHAT IS PLANNING? Planning means deciding in advance what you want to achieve and how you will achieve it. It involves setting goals, choosing actions, and arranging the resources and time needed. In short, planning helps you move from where you are now to where you want to be by thinking ahead and making smart decisions. KEY COMPONENTS OF PLANNING Objectives Clear, measurable goals for the organization Provide direction for all activities Policies General guidelines for decision-making Ensure consistency across departments Procedures Step-by-step methods to carry out tasks Standardizes workflow Rules Specific statements that must be followed No flexibility allowed Programs Comprehensive plan combining goals, policies, procedu Example: Training program, marketing program Budgets Numerical plans for allocating resources Helps in cost control and performance measurement Strategies Broad long-term plans to achieve competitive advantage Example: Market expansion, cost leadership NATURE OF PLANNING Goal-Oriented Focuses on achieving objectives Primary Function of Management Foundation for organizing, staffing, directing & controlling Pervasive Required at all levels: top, middle, and lower Continuous Process Plans must be updated according to change Future-Oriented Anticipates future conditions and prepares accordingly Decision-Making Activity Involves choosing the best alternative Flexible Adjusts to internal and external changes Integrated Coordinates different departments and resources LEVELS OF PLANNING Strategic Planning (Top Level) Long-term (5–10 years) Focus: Vision, mission, major goals Example: Expansion, diversification, mergers Tactical Planning (Middle Level) Medium-term (1–3 years) Converts strategic plans into actionable steps Example: Department-level goals & resource allocation Operational Planning (Lower Level) Short-term (daily/weekly/monthly) Ensures smooth day-to-day operations Example: Work schedules, shift planning, routine tasks ORGANIZING Fundamentals • Organization structure Design Fundamentals Definition Organizing is the process of identifying tasks, grouping them, assigning duties, and establishing authority relationships. Key Elements Job Identification: What tasks must be done Departmentation:

title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-20_Reference-Material-IV.pptx (Part 2)
content: Grouping tasks (HR, Finance, Marketing) Assignment of Duties: Who will do what Delegation of Authority: Granting power to perform tasks Coordination: Ensuring departments work together smoothly Importance Efficient use of resources Clarity in roles and responsibilities Reduces confusion and duplication Improves teamwork and productivity ORGANIZATION STRUCTURE Functional Structure Based on functions (Finance, HR, Production) Simple and efficient Best for small/mid-sized companies Divisional Structure Based on products, regions, or customer groups Each division operates like a small company Suitable for large diversified organizations Matrix Structure Combines functional & project structures Employees report to two managers Good for complex, innovative projects Team-Based Structure Organized around teams rather than hierarchy Fast decision-making and flexibility Network / Virtual Structure Core company outsources non-essential activities Highly flexible and cost-efficient ORGANIZATION DESIGN Definition Process of creating or modifying the structure of an organization to achieve strategic goals. title: Excerpt from WINSEM2025-26_BSTS102P_SS_CH2025260500131_2026-01-22_Reference-Material-I.pptx (Part 1)
content: Fundamental Counting Principle What is the Fundamental Counting Principle? The Fundamental Counting Principle (often called the Multiplication Rule) is a way of finding how many possibilities can exist when combining choices, objects, or results. This is done by multiplying each total choice count from each group being combined. Fundamental principle of counting is a rule used to find the total number of outcomes possible in a given situation. Fundamental principle of counting can be classified into two types Multiplication Rule (AND rule) Addition Rule (OR rule) Multiplication Rule If a certain work W can be completed by doing 2 tasks, first doing task A AND then doing task B. A can be done in m ways and following that B can be done in n ways, then the number of ways of doing the work W is (m x n) ways. Addition Rule If work W can be completed by doing task A OR task B, and A can be done in m ways and B can be done in n ways (and both cannot occur simultaneously: in this case we call tasks A and B as mutually exclusive), then work W can be done in (m + n) ways. For example, let’s say that a person can travel from New Delhi to Noida in 3 different types of buses, and 2 different types of trains, so, he can complete the work of going from New Delhi to Noida in 3 + 2 = 5 ways (As work can be completed by going by bus (A) OR by going by train (B)) A door on a classroom requires entry of 4 digits. All digits must be numbers, but the digits can not be repeated. How many unique codes are possible? 362880 40320 5040 720 QUESTION 1 Bakery has a selection

Circular arrangements are the type of permutations where the people or things are organized in a circle. In other terms, this arrangement is said to be circular . Rules of Circular Permutations The numerous methods to organize different items along a stable (i.e., not able to chosen up out of the even and spun over) circle is Pn = (n-1)!. The number is (n-1)! as a substitute for the normal factorial n! as all cyclic arrangements of items are equal since the circle can be swapped. Number of arrangements possible = (n − 1)! content: PROBABILITY-1 PROBABILITY: Probability is a measure of the likelihood that a specific event will occur. It is expressed as a number between 0 and 1, where 0 indicates that the event will not occur, and 1 indicates that the event will certainly occur. The probability of an event A is denoted as P(A). SAMPLE SPACE: The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. For example, if you roll a six-sided die, the sample space is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. RANDOM EXPERIMENT : An experiment in which all possible outcomes are known and the exact output cannot be be predicted in advance- tossing coins, rolling dice. EXAMPLE OF SAMPLE SPACE: 1 coin {H, T} = 2 2 coin {HH, TT, HT, TH} = 4 1 dice {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} = 6 SOME IMPORTANT POINTS OF PROBABILITY: Probability of sure event is 1. Probability of impossible event is 0. Any event 𝟎 ≤ 𝑷 ≤ 𝟏. The probability of any event varies from 0 to 1 P(no event occur) = 1- P( at least one event occur) PRACTICE QUESTIONS: 1. Independent Events: Two events are said to be independent if the occurrence or non- occurrence of one event does not influence the occurrence or non occurrence of the other event. Then probability of two events A & B is P(A)*P(B).
